key_insight: Bayesian inference applied to photoluminescence imaging and drift-diffusion modeling can localize where perovskite solar cells degrade, separating interface-driven failure from bulk degradation.
engineering_relevance: This enables targeted stability improvements in perovskite devices by revealing which interfaces or regions need passivation or process optimization.
method: Collect spatial photoluminescence images from fully fabricated perovskite solar cells.
Couple imaging data with drift-diffusion simulations and Bayesian inference to infer recombination-related parameter maps.
Track how these inferred maps evolve during aging at 70 °C under full-spectrum sunlight.
evidence_signal: The study reports temporally evolving inferred parameter maps and strong spatial heterogeneity, with the largest degradation traced to perovskite/transport-layer interfaces and reduced by amino-silane passivation.
watch_item: The approach depends on model assumptions in the drift-diffusion and inference framework, so inferred degradation locations may be sensitive to calibration quality and imaging coverage.
paper_title: Disentangling the origin of degradation in perovskite solar cells via optical imaging and Bayesian inference
